name: Personal Injury Plaintiff Fact Sheet Analyzer
summary: This skill extracts and normalizes medical provider, employment/wage loss, and insurance/lien data from a personal injury plaintiff's initial disclosures, Plaintiff Fact Sheets, and related discovery materials, then reconciles that data against draft builder responses to flag inconsistencies and supplementation needs. It produces builder-ready structured outputs with source traceability and a lawyer-facing issues memo designed to withstand adversarial scrutiny. The skill emphasizes conservative, citation-backed extraction, explicit uncertainty flags, entity verification against public registries, and mandatory attorney review to mitigate impeachment, sanctions, and confidentiality risks across federal and state jurisdictions.
content:
# Personal Injury Plaintiff Fact Sheet Analyzer: Strategic Data Extraction and Discovery Reconciliation

## 1. Opening Context

This skill produces a defensible, audit-ready extraction and reconciliation memo that pulls structured data from a personal injury plaintiff's initial disclosures and adjacent disclosure materials—including medical provider lists, employment information, and insurance data—and uses that data to populate and validate responses in a "builder" (typically a plaintiff fact sheet, interrogatory set, or case intake/discovery platform). In day-to-day practice, this work product sits at the seam between early case development and formal discovery: it is where incomplete intake, scattered medical records, and evolving treatment history are translated into sworn or attorney-certified discovery responses that opposing counsel will scrutinize for omissions and inconsistencies.

In personal injury litigation—particularly in mass torts, Multi-District Litigation (MDL), and complex catastrophic injury cases—the Plaintiff Fact Sheet (PFS) often serves as the foundational evidentiary document, functioning as a court-ordered surrogate for initial disclosures under Federal Rule of Civil Procedure 26(a)(1) or state equivalents. Done well, this analyzer reduces avoidable credibility damage (e.g., "You didn't disclose this provider," "You failed to identify a prior claim," "You omitted a lienholder"), prevents sanctions or motion practice over incomplete responses, and preserves strategic flexibility by clearly distinguishing what is known, what is uncertain, and what requires supplementation. Done poorly, it creates impeachment material, waiver arguments, spoliation narratives, HIPAA and authorization confusion, and malpractice exposure—especially where an omission later appears intentional. Professional standards governing this workflow include the duty of competence and diligence in factual investigation (ABA Model Rules 1.1, 1.3), confidentiality (Model Rule 1.6), truthfulness and candor in court-connected submissions (Model Rules 3.3, 3.4, 4.1), and the litigation duty to timely supplement discovery responses where required under governing procedural rules such as FRCP 26(e) and state analogs.

## 2. Understanding the Context and Purpose

Personal injury cases live and die on records: medical causation, damages, prior conditions, wage loss, and collateral source issues are largely documentary. Plaintiff "initial disclosures" (in federal court, typically FRCP 26(a)(1) disclosures) and early disclosure packets (provider lists, authorizations, medical specials summaries) form the baseline narrative that later discovery responses must track. The PFS is the "source of truth" against which all future testimony and records will be measured. Opposing counsel uses the baseline to test credibility, frame record subpoenas, build alternative causation and preexisting-condition defenses, identify collateral source payments and liens, and evaluate settlement value.

This analyzer's strategic role is twofold. First, it is an extraction engine: it pulls names, addresses, dates of service, claim numbers, policy limits (if disclosed), wage information, and related identifiers from disclosure materials and converts them into normalized fields that the builder can reuse. Second, it is a reconciliation engine: it compares extracted data against what the builder is about to say, flags inconsistencies, and prompts supplementation language (e.g., "Plaintiff continues to treat and will supplement") where permissible and accurate.

The primary readers and downstream users are not just the plaintiff's litigation team. The output anticipates hostile review by defense counsel and their record retrieval vendors, as well as scrutiny by mediators, carriers, and—if responses are verified—by the plaintiff personally under oath. The output will also be relied upon by paralegals for subpoena preparation, by associates for drafting discovery, and by partners for assessing settlement value based on the documented breadth of treatment. Accordingly, the analyzer must be conservative: it should never "invent" a provider, date, employer, or policy term. It should surface uncertainty, propose follow-up questions, and preserve traceability to the underlying disclosure text.

Scope boundary: this skill is designed to extract and reconcile facts from existing disclosure materials and prepare structured output and issue flags. It does not give legal advice on claim valuation, does not determine admissibility, and does not replace attorney judgment about privilege, work product, or strategic disclosure sequencing.

## 3. Prerequisites and Information Gathering

Before performing substantive extraction, the executing LLM must obtain and review the complete set of disclosure materials that the builder responses are supposed to match. If any of the following are missing, the LLM must stop and ask the user to provide them, or clearly label the output as "Partial," identifying exactly what is missing and how that absence limits confidence.

At minimum, collect the following: (a) the executed Plaintiff Fact Sheet or initial disclosures (federal: FRCP 26(a)(1) packet; state: the jurisdiction's equivalent, if used; MDL: the PFS mandated by the Case Management Order), including any attachments; (b) any separate medical provider list, medical specials spreadsheet, treatment chronology, or HIPAA authorization list sent to defense; (c) any wage loss package (employer verification, disability notes, pay stubs, W-2s, tax returns if produced, employer contact sheets); (d) any insurance and benefits disclosures (health insurance cards, Explanation of Benefits summaries, PIP/MedPay declarations, lien letters, subrogation notices, Medicare/Medicaid status letters, insurance declaration pages); (e) client intake notes or questionnaires (internal firm documents that may contain raw details omitted from the formal PFS); and (f) the current draft builder responses the system intends to populate or validate.

Because personal injury disclosures evolve quickly, the LLM should also request: the date range the disclosures cover (through what date were providers "current"?); the incident date(s) and any prior injury history that has been disclosed; whether any treatment was cash-pay, under a letter of protection, or through workers' compensation; a complete list of all pharmacies used in the relevant time period; whether there are any gaps in the employment timeline exceeding six months; and whether there are known undisclosed providers the firm is aware of but intentionally not yet disclosed (if so, the attorney must decide strategy; the LLM should not conceal facts).

The LLM must also ask for the governing forum and jurisdiction (federal vs. specific state court), because supplementation duties and disclosure regimes vary materially. In federal MDLs, the court often adopts a specific Case Management Order (CMO) that mandates the exact format of the PFS; the LLM must be instructed to follow CMO-mandated headers exactly, as failure to do so can result in a Notice of Non-Compliance and potential dismissal.

## 4. Step-by-Step Process

### Step 1: Establish the Source-of-Truth Record and Build a Traceable Extraction Map

Begin by treating the disclosure packet as the controlling source of truth for what was actually communicated. The LLM's job is not to reconstruct the plaintiff's entire medical and employment history from memory; it is to faithfully extract what exists in the documents, with citations to where each data point came from. Create a traceable extraction map: for each item extracted (provider name, address, dates, employer, policy number), record the exact page, line, or document section it came from.

This step matters because the most common failure mode is a later challenge—"You said X in disclosures, now you say Y in interrogatories"—and the team needs to immediately show what the source document actually said. Traceability is also the principal anti-hallucination guardrail: every extracted fact must be tied to specific text in the source documents. If a fact cannot be traced to a source, it must not be presented as extracted data.

Normalize document references consistently. If the disclosure PDF is paginated, use "Doc 1, p. 4." If it is a Word file, use headings and paragraph numbers ("Provider List §2"). If the documents are in an e-discovery platform, use Bates ranges ("P000123–P000126"). Where the source is ambiguous (e.g., multiple provider lists exist), label the version by date. The following is an illustrative extraction notation: "Dr. Jane Smith Orthopedics — identified in Initial Disclosures, Provider List dated 2025-01-12, p. 2 (Bates P000045)."

Common mistakes at this stage include extracting provider names without capturing addresses when the builder requires them; mixing "treatment facility" and "billing entity" without noting both; silently correcting spelling or guessing expansions of abbreviations; and merging duplicate-looking entities without confirming they are the same (e.g., "ABC Imaging" vs. "ABC Radiology LLC"). Defense counsel will use omissions or "moving targets" to argue bad faith. The traceable map should allow an attorney to defend a correction as a good-faith clarification rather than a concealment.

In federal practice, initial disclosures have defined categories and supplementation obligations under FRCP 26(a)(1) and 26(e). Many state courts do not have the same initial disclosure structure; instead, early disclosures may be informal or driven by standing orders. The analyzer must therefore treat the packet as factual history of what was communicated, regardless of whether the disclosures are formally denominated as "Rule 26" disclosures.

### Step 2: Parse and Normalize Medical Provider Information

Medical provider extraction requires capturing the attributes that matter in discovery: identity (who), location (where), time period (when), and relationship to injury (why it matters). A competent provider list distinguishes at minimum among three categories: injury-related treatment providers (ER, specialists, PT, imaging, surgery); preexisting-condition or prior injury providers that have been disclosed (often central to causation defenses); and non-treatment entities tied to billing and liens (ambulance services, separately billed radiology reads, pathology, facility fees). Additionally, the LLM must separately identify pharmacies and diagnostic facilities (MRI centers, labs), which are frequently overlooked but essential for complete discovery.

Scan the disclosure materials for any section labeled "medical providers," "treaters," "facilities," "records," "authorizations," "specials," or "medical bills." Extract every entity mentioned, even if the disclosure says "records requested" or "to be provided." For each entity, capture: full facility or provider name, provider type (hospital, clinic, individual physician, PT, imaging center, pharmacy), address, phone, and fax if present, dates of service or date range, National Provider Identifier (NPI) if available, and whether records and bills were disclosed or merely identified.

Normalization matters because builder fields usually require consistent formatting. Preserve the exact disclosed name in a "verbatim_name" field, and create a "normalized_name" field for deduplication and builder population. The following is an illustrative builder-population record: "Providence ER (facility) — 03/14/2024 (date of incident) — address per disclosure: 123 Main St., Anytown, ST 00000 — records: identified, production pending."

A critical component that distinguishes strategic extraction from mechanical listing is linking providers to specific injuries claimed in the litigation. If a provider is listed for a broken toe in 2010 but the case concerns a back injury in 2023, the LLM must still extract the data but categorize it as "Potential Pre-existing/Prior History." This categorization is essential because defense counsel will exploit any failure to disclose prior treatment as evidence of concealment.

Common mistakes include assuming date ranges where only a single visit is listed; failing to capture radiology or pathology separate billers; treating a referral as an actual treatment encounter; collapsing multiple locations of a provider network into one; and producing a PFS that only lists a primary care doctor when the treatment history is far broader. Defense vendors will subpoena by entity name and address; if the extraction omits an address or misidentifies the correct corporate entity, record retrieval delays occur and defense counsel may insinuate noncooperation.

Some jurisdictions require early disclosure of treating physicians intended to testify (state-specific expert disclosure regimes) versus mere identification of providers. This analyzer should not decide who qualifies as an expert. Instead, it should extract what is disclosed and flag where a provider might later be designated as a non-retained treating expert, which triggers separate deadlines and disclosures often governed by state rules or FRCP 26(a)(2).

### Step 3: Extract Employment, Wage Loss, and Work Capacity Information

Employment information is frequently internally inconsistent across intake, disclosures, and wage loss packages: job titles drift, employer entities are misnamed, and date ranges change when plaintiffs recall additional details. Treat wage loss as a damages component requiring careful factual foundation grounded in "but-for" causation: but for the injury, would the plaintiff still be employed at the disclosed rate?

Identify every employer mentioned in the disclosures and attachments. Extract: legal employer name (as disclosed), any "doing business as" name, employer address, supervisor or HR contact if provided, plaintiff job title, employment start and end dates (if any), work schedule, pay rate (hourly or salary), and wage documentation disclosed (pay stubs, W-2s, tax returns, employer letters). Also capture the injury's asserted impact: first missed work date, total days or hours missed, whether PTO was used, and whether the plaintiff returned to full duty or light duty.

The LLM must identify any "employment gaps." In the adversarial context, defense counsel will scrutinize gaps to argue that the plaintiff's loss of income was due to preexisting economic factors, poor performance, or voluntary departure rather than the injury. Where the disclosure materials are vague (e.g., "Plaintiff missed about two months"), the output must preserve the vagueness rather than fabricate precision. Present it as "reported estimate per disclosure" and flag as requiring client verification.

The following is an illustrative population entry: "Employer: Acme Logistics LLC (verbatim per Initial Disclosures p. 6). Position: warehouse associate. Pay: '$18/hour' (verbatim). Time missed: 'approximately 6 weeks' following 03/14/2024 incident; exact dates not specified; follow-up needed to confirm first missed date and return date. Strategic Note: Plaintiff claims total disability post-DOI; verify whether Short-Term Disability (STD) was applied for through this employer."

Common mistakes include converting approximate time periods into exact dates; assuming the employer is the same as the worksite (staffing agencies are frequent in warehouse and construction cases); failing to capture self-employment details; and ignoring mitigation issues (job search, return to work) that affect damages. Wage loss claims are heavily attacked; defense will request payroll records, tax returns, and disability applications, and any inconsistencies become impeachment material. The reconciliation should flag mismatches between wage loss claimed in the builder and what was previously disclosed.

Some states limit recoverable wage loss or treat collateral benefits differently under varying collateral source rules. The analyzer should not opine on admissibility or offsets; it should extract benefits information and flag for attorney evaluation.

### Step 4: Extract Insurance, Benefits, Liens, and Prior Claims

Insurance information in personal injury cases includes multiple layers: plaintiff health insurance (private, Medicare, Medicaid), auto insurance (PIP, MedPay, UIM), liability policies (if known), workers' compensation, disability insurance, and lien or subrogation interests. Builder questions often ask for carrier name, policy number, group number, claim number, adjuster contact, and whether liens exist.

The key legal risk here is overstatement. Do not guess policy limits, coverage applicability, or lien validity. Extract only what is present in the documents. Locate any insurance cards, declarations pages, Explanations of Benefits, lien letters, and correspondence with carriers. Extract: insurer name, plan type (health, auto, workers' compensation, disability), member ID, group number, claim number, policy number (if present), named insured (if stated), and contact information. For lien interests, extract the lienholder identity, notice date, amount claimed (if stated), and whether the lien is "asserted," "pending," or "final."

Medicare and Medicaid status is particularly sensitive. If disclosures include a Medicare beneficiary letter or conditional payment notice, capture the status and reference document date, but do not provide compliance conclusions. Where disclosures are silent on Medicare status, flag the need to confirm whether the plaintiff is a Medicare beneficiary or has applied for Social Security Disability Income (SSDI), because that may trigger Medicare Secondary Payer reporting and repayment workflows.

The following is an illustrative extraction entry: "Health insurer: BlueCross BlueShield (card image, Doc 3). Member ID: XXXXX. Group: XXXXX. No lien letter produced as of disclosure date 2025-01-12; supplementation likely if subrogation asserted."

Common mistakes include copying partial numbers incorrectly; confusing claim number with policy number; treating a provider's letter of protection as an insurance lien (it is typically a contractual payment arrangement, not an insurance product); and failing to capture that bills were paid by PIP or MedPay. Defense counsel will often serve Requests for Production for the actual insurance policies to check for Coordination of Benefits clauses; the LLM must ensure the PFS data matches the policy declaration pages exactly.

Collateral source and lien issues affect settlement strategy and net recovery. If the disclosure materials mention "prior accident," "prior claim," or "prior settlement," extract the information and flag it for consistency with the builder's answers. In no-fault jurisdictions, PIP carrier details and treating-provider billing relationships become central; in fault-based jurisdictions, health insurance and liens may be the main focus. The analyzer should prompt the user to specify whether the jurisdiction is no-fault or PIP-based and whether MedPay is involved, then tailor which insurance subfields are considered required in the builder.

### Step 5: Reconcile Extracted Data Against the Builder Draft and Generate Variance Flags

Once extraction is complete, perform a reconciliation pass against the builder's current draft responses. This is where the analyzer earns its primary value: it surfaces mismatches before they become admissions. Treat reconciliation as a "diff" exercise across three dimensions: completeness (is anything missing from the builder that appears in the disclosures, or vice versa), consistency (do names, dates, and amounts match), and characterization (is the builder overstating or understating what the disclosures support).

For completeness, compare provider lists entity-by-entity. If the builder lists fewer providers than the disclosures, flag "missing provider in builder." If the builder lists more providers than disclosures, flag "new provider not in disclosures—confirm supplementation posture." For consistency, check spelling variations, address changes, and date ranges. For characterization, if the builder states "no prior injuries" but disclosures mention prior treatment, flag it as a high-risk inconsistency.

The LLM must also look for contradictions between documents. If the PFS lists "Dr. Jones" as the primary surgeon, but hospital records show "Dr. Miller" performed the surgery, this is a critical discrepancy. Similarly, if the PFS states the plaintiff has not worked since June 2023, but a May 2024 medical record mentions the plaintiff "complained of back pain while lifting crates at work," this is a high-priority "Red Alert" requiring immediate attorney intervention before discovery responses are served.

Output should include a variance narrative that an attorney can use immediately. The following is an illustrative variance entry: "Variance: Builder Q12 states Plaintiff treated only with 'Downtown PT' from 04/01–05/01. Disclosures identify additional imaging at 'ABC Imaging' on 03/20 and orthopedic consult with 'Dr. Jane Smith' on 04/10 (Provider List p. 2). Recommend updating Q12 to include these treaters or confirming whether they were disclosed in a later supplement."

Common mistakes include "fixing" the builder by silently overwriting without showing the variance; failing to distinguish between disclosed-but-not-produced records and entirely undisclosed providers; and assuming the builder should match the disclosures even when the attorney intentionally narrowed scope (strategy must be attorney-driven, not model-driven). Defense counsel will often attach prior disclosures as exhibits in a motion to compel or to impeach at deposition. Variance flags should prioritize deposition-impeachment risks: undisclosed prior accidents, gaps in treatment, and inconsistencies in employment and wage loss.

Supplementation timing differs by jurisdiction. In federal court, FRCP 26(e) requires supplementation in a timely manner if the disclosure is incomplete or incorrect in a material respect. Some state regimes impose stricter deadlines or require verified responses. The reconciliation should therefore include a "timing sensitivity" note whenever missing items likely require prompt supplementation.

### Step 6: Produce Builder-Ready Structured Output and a Lawyer-Facing Issues Memo

Deliver two layers of output. First, structured data formatted exactly as the builder needs (e.g., JSON fields, table rows, or keyed sections matching the builder's schema). Second, a lawyer-facing issues memo explaining what was extracted, what is uncertain, and what needs attorney or client confirmation.

The structured output should separate "verbatim" from "normalized" fields to preserve fidelity. It should also track a "source_citation" for each record and a "confidence" label that is evidence-based: "high" means explicitly listed with address and date; "medium" means entity listed but missing dates or address; "low" means inferred from context and requires verification. Do not use confidence as a proxy for guessing; it is a signal of source completeness. The structured output must map to the specific fields required by the firm's builder, whether that is "Exhibit A: Provider List" or "Interrogatory No. 4: Employment History." If the builder requires separate fields for facility versus individual provider, do not combine them. If the builder requires one employment entry per employer, do not merge separate entities.

The issues memo should read like a discovery-preparation note. It should highlight: required follow-up questions for the client (e.g., missing employer dates, unknown insurance status, pharmacy history); potential supplementation needs with timing sensitivity; likely defense focus areas and impeachment vectors; and any red flags (undisclosed prior claims, inconsistent injury mechanism descriptions, treatment gaps, employment gaps). The memo should also include appropriate legal caveats in the builder-ready responses, such as "Plaintiff continues to treat and reserves the right to supplement this response as discovery continues."

The following is an illustrative issues memo entry: "Records indicate treatment at two facilities with similar names (ABC Imaging vs. ABC Radiology LLC). Confirm whether these are the same entity before authorizations are issued. Builder currently lists only ABC Imaging; risk of subpoena misdirection and impeachment if discrepancy appears in deposition exhibits."

Common mistakes include producing only a populated builder output without explaining uncertainties; failing to preserve source traceability; or using conclusory legal language (e.g., "no liens exist") when the record only shows "no lien letters received yet." If the output will be used in a jurisdiction that requires verification by the plaintiff, elevate the standard: every factual assertion must be traceable to a source or flagged for client verification.

Regarding privilege: while the extraction outputs are largely factual compilations, the issues memo reflects attorney strategy. Clearly label the issues memo as "DRAFT — Attorney Work Product / For Counsel Review Only" and instruct that an attorney decide storage and sharing practices. Privilege protection is jurisdiction-dependent and fact-sensitive; labeling does not guarantee protection.

## 5. Critical Legal Standards and Compliance

This workflow implicates professional responsibility and procedural compliance because it supports discovery responses and representations to opposing counsel.

The duty of competence under ABA Model Rule 1.1 requires thorough and accurate preparation of discovery documents. Reliance on an LLM for data extraction does not absolve the attorney of the duty to verify the accuracy of the underlying data. The duty of diligence under Model Rule 1.3 demands adequate factual investigation and careful handling of source materials. The LLM must not "fill in the blanks" or average across inconsistent documents; doing so creates false statements.

Confidentiality under Model Rule 1.6 is central because disclosure packets contain medical and insurance identifiers. Treat all inputs as confidential client information and avoid unnecessary reproduction of full identifiers in outputs. Where possible, mask sensitive numbers (e.g., last four digits) in the lawyer-facing memo while preserving full values only in secure, builder-required fields.

Candor and truthfulness matter because extracted information often feeds sworn discovery responses or attorney certifications. Model Rule 3.3 (Candor Toward the Tribunal) means that knowingly submitting a PFS or discovery response that omits material medical history can lead to sanctions under FRCP 37 or state equivalents. Model Rule 3.4 (Fairness to Opposing Party and Counsel) prohibits unlawful obstruction of another party's access to evidence; incomplete provider lists are frequently cited in sanctions motions under this rule. Model Rule 4.1 (Truthfulness in Statements to Others) prohibits false statements of material fact. The analyzer should therefore distinguish "documented facts" from "reported estimates," avoid definitive statements where the record is incomplete, and prompt supplementation where rules require it.

Privilege awareness extends beyond the issues memo. While the facts of treatment are not privileged, the LLM must be careful not to include attorney-client communications that may have been inadvertently included in intake notes. If such communications are identified, flag them and exclude them from the extraction output.

HIPAA compliance governs the handling of Protected Health Information. All data extracted constitutes PHI. The LLM must operate within a secure, compliant environment, and the final output must be handled according to firm data privacy protocols. The analyzer should not generate medical authorizations as a substitute for counsel; it should extract the provider data needed to prepare lawful record requests and authorizations accurately, and flag that state-specific medical authorization requirements must be confirmed by the attorney.

Conflicts: this skill does not itself run conflict checks. If documents indicate multiple potential clients (e.g., spouse passenger, multiple claimants), flag for attorney conflict screening under Model Rule 1.7.

Malpractice risk areas include missing providers (leading to sanctions or impeachment), misstating wage loss (inflating damages and undermining credibility), mishandling lien and subrogation information (impairing settlement), and exposing PHI beyond what is necessary. Mitigation is achieved through traceability, conservative phrasing, explicit uncertainty flags, and mandatory attorney review.

## 6. Quality Control and Review

Quality control for this skill is not proofreading; it is litigation defensibility. The following framework should be applied before any output is finalized.

First, validate source traceability: every populated builder field must have a supporting source citation or be clearly labeled "client to confirm" or "requires supplementation." Re-check that governing law references are accurate: if the output references FRCP obligations, ensure the case is actually in federal court; otherwise label as "state analog—attorney to confirm." Any legal-rule references require attorney verification.

Second, verify entity existence. Use the CMS NPI Registry (https://npiregistry.cms.hhs.gov/) or equivalent web search to confirm that provider names and addresses actually exist. Hallucinated facility names are a high-risk failure mode for LLM-generated outputs. Where a provider name cannot be verified against a public registry, flag it for manual confirmation.

Third, test date consistency. Do the treatment dates align with the Date of Incident? Any treatment before the DOI must be clearly marked as "Prior." Confirm that date formats are consistent throughout and that the LLM did not convert estimates into false specifics. Confirm that identifiers (policy numbers, claim numbers) are transcribed accurately; where the source is an image, flag potential OCR error and require human double-check.

Fourth, evaluate completeness. Does the extraction include pharmacies, physical therapists, and diagnostic imaging, or only physicians? A PFS that lists only a primary care doctor is likely deficient. Randomly sample extracted providers and confirm they appear in the source documents exactly as stated.

Fifth, apply adversarial scrutiny. Ask: if defense counsel compared the builder responses side-by-side with the initial disclosures, would any omission or discrepancy look like concealment? Where discrepancies exist, has the output either corrected the builder or flagged the discrepancy with recommended clarification language? Prioritize impeachment vectors such as a ten-year gap in treatment, a job transition just before the accident, or undisclosed prior claims.

Sixth, ensure output usability. Builder-ready data must match the builder's expected schema and constraints. If the builder requires separate fields for facility versus provider, do not combine them.

All output requires attorney review before use, service, or client signature. This analyzer is an assistive drafting and extraction tool and does not constitute legal advice.

## 7. Jurisdiction-Specific Adaptations

This skill is designed to be jurisdiction-aware but not jurisdiction-assumptive. The first adaptation decision is whether the matter is in federal court (triggering FRCP 26(a)(1) initial disclosures and FRCP 26(e) supplementation duties) or in a specific state forum.

In federal MDL practice, courts often adopt Case Management Orders that mandate the exact format and content of the PFS. The LLM must follow CMO-mandated headers and categories exactly. Non-compliance can result in administrative dismissal. The analyzer should expect structured initial disclosures and should treat supplementation as an ongoing obligation tied to material completeness.

In many state cases, "initial disclosures" may be informal exchanges or required by local standing orders. The analyzer should treat them as communications that can still be used for impeachment. Verified discovery responses represent another key divergence: some states require verification for interrogatories or plaintiff fact sheets, while others allow attorney-signed responses. Where verification is required, the analyzer should elevate flags for any uncertainty and recommend client confirmation before finalization.

The following are illustrative jurisdiction-specific considerations that demonstrate the type of adaptation required. In California, the Code of Civil Procedure governs discovery, and responses require a specific verification signed by the plaintiff; the LLM should remind the user that a verification form must be generated alongside the data extract. In Florida, standard interrogatories for personal injury under Form 1.977 of the Florida Rules of Civil Procedure are often used as the baseline; the LLM should align its extraction categories with that form's requirements, such as listing all healthcare providers seen in the past ten years. In Texas, Required Disclosures under Rule 194 of the Texas Rules of Civil Procedure impose specific categories of information; the LLM must ensure the extraction supports those categories. These examples are illustrative and not exhaustive; the LLM must always confirm current rules with the supervising attorney for the applicable jurisdiction.

Medical privacy and authorization practices also vary by state. Some states have specific statutory authorization forms or restrictions on ex parte provider contact. The analyzer should not recommend contacting providers; it should limit itself to extracting the information needed to prepare lawful record requests and authorizations, and it should flag "state-specific medical authorization requirements—attorney to confirm."

Collateral source, lien treatment, and PIP/no-fault regimes materially affect which insurance fields matter. In no-fault states, PIP carrier details and treating-provider billing relationships become central; in fault-based jurisdictions, health insurance and liens may be the main focus. The analyzer should prompt the user to specify the applicable regime and tailor required insurance subfields accordingly.

Because this skill is designed for production use across jurisdictions, any statement about procedural obligations or statutory regimes must be either supported by a jurisdiction-specific source confirmed via web verification at runtime, or clearly flagged for attorney confirmation. The LLM must verify all cited legal rules and standards through available search tools before including them in final output; where verification is not possible, the output must note the specific claim as unverified and requiring attorney review.

---
*Disclaimer: This skill is a tool for legal professionals. It does not provide legal advice. All outputs—especially medical and employment chronologies, insurance data, and populated discovery responses—must be verified against original source documents by a licensed attorney before being served on opposing counsel or filed with a court.*
